worth_it_explanation: Child-directed treatment is a legal line, not a yield setting, so the cost of getting it wrong is regulatory rather than a few points of revenue. Under COPPA, inventory that reaches children under 13 cannot carry personalized advertising or the data collection that feeds it. Microsoft Monetize gives a publisher a placement-level control that applies COPPA rules and suppresses user data collection once a placement is marked as intended for children, so the check is a review of how that control is set against the inventory that actually reaches minors. The review costs reading time against the placement list and the audience the content serves, and the finding points to a concrete compliance gap a publisher can close before it becomes an enforcement matter.
intro: The Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) restricts how online services collect and use the personal information of children under 13, and on the supply side that restriction lands on the inventory a publisher monetizes. Microsoft Monetize (formerly Xandr Monetize) carries this through a placement-level intended-audience control. A placement marked as intended for children triggers special COPPA rules in the system, including that user information is not collected and line items that need that information to serve are held off. This subcategory is about whether that control is set to match the inventory that actually reaches children, and whether restricted data processing follows through on inventory likely to reach minors. These checks confirm that the COPPA treatment reflects the audience the content serves rather than a default that was never reviewed.

audit_checks.title: Child-directed placements are not flagged as intended for children
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a placement that reaches children under 13 is not marked as intended for children, Microsoft Monetize does not apply the COPPA rules that suppress personal data collection, so personalized advertising and the data uses behind it continue on inventory the law protects. That is a direct compliance breach with regulatory exposure under COPPA rather than a revenue trade, which is why the impact sits at the top of the scale.

educate.base:
The Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) governs how online services collect and use personal information from children under 13. On the supply side, the obligation attaches to inventory that reaches those children, so a publisher monetizing child-directed content carries the duty to keep personalized advertising and the data collection behind it off that inventory. The Federal Trade Commission (FTC), which enforces COPPA, treats the rule as reaching not only sites built for children but also general-audience services that have actual knowledge they are collecting information from a child under 13.

Microsoft Monetize (formerly Xandr Monetize) carries COPPA treatment through a placement-level control. When a placement is self-classified with an intended audience of children, the system applies special COPPA rules, including that user information is not collected, and line items that need user information for frequency capping or other targeting will not serve on the placement. The control sits in the categories and auditing section of the placement setup, so the COPPA outcome depends on a setting the publisher chooses placement by placement.

The gap this check looks for is inventory that reaches children but carries no child-directed flag. A placement left at the default general-audience classification continues to take personalized demand and the data uses behind it because the system has no signal that COPPA rules should apply. The audience the content serves and the classification on the placement can drift apart, for example when a kids-content section launches under an existing publisher account and the new placements inherit the default classification.

A few signs point to child-directed inventory that is not flagged:
  • Placements serving content aimed at children under 13 carry the default general-audience classification rather than the children intended-audience setting
  • The publisher cannot point to a review that matched the intended-audience classification against the content each placement serves
  • A new child-directed section or property was added without a corresponding pass over its placements' COPPA settings
You may want to confirm that every placement reaching children under 13 carries the children intended-audience classification so the COPPA rules apply. One approach is to walk the placement list against the content each placement serves and reclassify any child-directed placement that sits at the default.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a children's section such as a games hub or a kids' video area can sit alongside general-audience editorial under one publisher account. You may want to confirm that the placements inside the child-directed section carry the children intended-audience classification even though the rest of the site does not, so the COPPA rules apply where they are owed and not where they are not.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a category or guide aimed at children, for example a toys section or a kids' product guide, can reach an audience the rest of the catalog does not. You may want to check whether the placements on those child-directed pages are flagged as intended for children because the COPPA obligation follows the audience of the content rather than the commercial nature of the page.

learn_more.source_title: Children's Online Privacy Protection Rule: A Six-Step Compliance Plan for Your Business

investigate.intro: Review the intended-audience classification on each placement against the content it serves, and identify any placement reaching children under 13 that is not flagged as intended for children.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize UI
steps: Open 'Publishers', then 'Inventory Manager', and select the publisher to list its placements.
Open each placement and check the 'Categories and auditing' section for the intended-audience classification, noting whether it is set to children or left at the general-audience default.
List the content each placement serves and mark any placement whose content reaches children under 13.
Record any placement that serves child-directed content but is not classified as intended for children. Those placements are the ones missing the COPPA flag.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per placement, the intended-audience classification and whether the content the placement serves reaches children under 13. A setup where every placement serving child-directed content carries the children intended-audience classification passes. A setup where a placement reaching children under 13 sits at the general-audience default is a fail recorded with the placement and the content it serves. The COPPA rules are then not applied to inventory the law protects.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the intended-audience classification and the content served for each placement above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} serves child-directed content on placements that are not flagged as intended for children in Microsoft Monetize, so the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) rules that suppress personal data collection are not being applied. A placement left at the general-audience default continues to take personalized demand and the data uses behind it, which on inventory reaching children under 13 is a compliance breach rather than a revenue choice. {{voice_recommend}} reclassifying every placement that serves child-directed content with the children intended-audience setting so the system applies the COPPA rules and holds back line items that need user information. After the change, recheck the placement list against the content each placement serves to confirm the classification matches the audience.

audit_checks.title: Restricted data processing does not cover inventory likely to reach minors
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Inventory that is likely to reach minors, rather than being squarely built for children, sits in a gray zone where the COPPA obligation can still attach but the child-directed flag is easy to leave off. When restricted data processing does not extend to that inventory, personal data uses continue on audiences the rule may protect, which carries real regulatory exposure even though it is a step less clear-cut than an outright child-directed placement left unflagged.

educate.base:
Not all inventory that reaches children is built for children. A general-audience property can carry a section, a format, or a slice of content that draws a meaningful share of minors without being directed to them in the way a dedicated kids' site is. The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) frames the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) as reaching general-audience services once they have actual knowledge they are collecting personal information from a child under 13, so the obligation can attach to inventory that was never classified as child-directed.

Restricted data processing is the practice of holding back the personal data uses that drive personalized advertising on inventory where they should not run. In Microsoft Monetize (formerly Xandr Monetize), the placement-level intended-audience control is the mechanism that does this for child-directed inventory. Marking a placement as intended for children suppresses user information collection and holds back line items that need that information. The audit question for gray-zone inventory is whether that suppression reaches the placements likely to serve minors, not only the placements obviously built for them.

The risk is a coverage gap. A publisher may flag the dedicated kids' section while leaving the borderline inventory, for example a family-content area or a format that skews young, on the default classification. Restricted data processing then covers the clear cases and misses the gray-zone inventory where the COPPA obligation can still attach. The data uses behind personalized advertising continue on an audience the rule may protect, and the publisher carries the exposure without a record of having weighed the inventory.

A few signs point to restricted data processing that does not cover inventory likely to reach minors:
  • Only inventory built squarely for children carries the children intended-audience classification, while borderline inventory likely to reach minors sits at the general-audience default
  • No record exists of which inventory was assessed for whether it is likely to reach children under 13
  • A format or section that skews toward a young audience takes personalized demand with no restricted data processing applied
You may want to confirm that the inventory likely to reach minors, and not only the inventory built for children, is assessed for child-directed treatment. One approach is to review the audience each section and format actually reaches, then apply the children intended-audience classification to the placements where minors make up a meaningful share.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a family-content section or a format such as cartoons or all-ages video can draw a young audience without being labeled a children's product. You may want to assess those gray-zone areas for whether they are likely to reach children under 13 and apply restricted data processing through the children intended-audience classification where they do, rather than limiting the treatment to a dedicated kids' section.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, content around toys, games, or family products can reach minors even when the surrounding catalog is general-audience. You may want to review whether those placements are likely to reach children and extend restricted data processing to them because the COPPA obligation can attach to the audience of the content regardless of the commercial framing.

learn_more.source_title: Complying with COPPA: Frequently Asked Questions

investigate.intro: Review which inventory has been assessed for whether it is likely to reach children under 13, and check whether restricted data processing through the children intended-audience classification covers that gray-zone inventory or only the inventory built squarely for children.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize UI
steps: Open 'Publishers', then 'Inventory Manager', and list the placements with their intended-audience classification from the 'Categories and auditing' section.
Identify the sections, formats, and placements that are likely to reach minors without being built squarely for children, such as family content or formats that skew young.
Record whether each of those gray-zone placements carries the children intended-audience classification or sits at the general-audience default.
Ask the publisher whether a record exists of which inventory was assessed for likelihood of reaching children under 13, and note what the assessment covered and what it left out.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per placement that is likely to reach minors, whether it carries the children intended-audience classification and whether the inventory was assessed for the likelihood of reaching children under 13. A setup where the gray-zone inventory has been assessed and the placements likely to reach minors carry restricted data processing passes. A setup where only inventory built squarely for children is flagged, while borderline inventory likely to reach minors takes personalized demand at the default classification, is a fail recorded with the placements and sections left uncovered.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record, for each placement likely to reach minors, its intended-audience classification and whether the inventory was assessed above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that restricted data processing at {{site_name}} covers the inventory built squarely for children but not the inventory likely to reach minors, such as family content and formats that skew young, which sits at the general-audience default. The Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) can attach to general-audience services that reach children under 13, so leaving that gray-zone inventory on personalized demand carries regulatory exposure. {{voice_recommend}} assessing each section and format for whether it is likely to reach children under 13, then applying the children intended-audience classification in Microsoft Monetize to the placements where minors make up a meaningful share so restricted data processing follows the audience. After the change, keep a record of which inventory was assessed and how it was treated.
